title: The floppy infant with lactate that won't come down
blurb: Pyruvate piles up at the gate of the mitochondrion, spilling to lactate and alanine — and a ketogenic diet, paradoxically, helps.

patient.complaint: Poor feeding, hypotonia, and episodes of rapid breathing.

opening: 4-month-old boy with hypotonia since birth, poor head control, and intermittent tachypnea. Bedside glucose is normal but venous lactate is 7.8 and persists despite hydration. Plasma alanine is high; MRI shows thin corpus callosum and basal ganglia changes. The lactate does NOT fall with glucose — it rises. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Born at term; hypotonia and feeding difficulty from the first weeks. Mild dysmorphism noted. No fever or sepsis. Parents non-consanguineous (the common form is X-linked, PDHA1). Older female cousin with developmental delay.
exam: Hypotonic with poor head control, brisk-ish reflexes, intermittent tachypnea (compensating for acidosis). Narrow forehead, mild dysmorphism. Liver not enlarged. No organomegaly.

labs.name: Brain MRI
labs.value: Thin corpus callosum, basal ganglia signal

prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why does a glucose load make it WORSE, and why does a ketogenic diet help?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the step that connects glycolysis to the TCA cycle and fails here.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — pyruvate dehydrogenase. Pyruvate can't be converted to acetyl-CoA, so it backs up to lactate (acidosis) and alanine. Glucose feeds more pyruvate into the jam (worsens lactate); a KETOGENIC diet supplies acetyl-CoA directly from fat, bypassing the block.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The block is at the bridge from pyruvate into the mitochondrial TCA cycle.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: PDH (Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA). It is a thiamine/lipoate/FAD/NAD/CoA complex — the same family as α-KGDH and BCKDH.

mcqs.stem: Why does a glucose load worsen the lactate in PDH deficiency?
choices: More glucose → more pyruvate, which cannot proceed to acetyl-CoA and is shunted to lactate
Glucose inhibits the kidney's lactate clearance
mcqs.explanation: With the PDH gate shut, extra carbohydrate just feeds more pyruvate into a dead end → more lactate and alanine. This is why carbohydrate loading worsens PDH deficiency, opposite to glycogen storage disease.

mcqs.stem: Why is a ketogenic diet the cornerstone of treatment?
choices: Fat-derived acetyl-CoA (via β-oxidation and ketones) enters the TCA cycle downstream of the block
mcqs.explanation: Ketone bodies and β-oxidation generate acetyl-CoA distal to PDH, fueling the TCA cycle without needing pyruvate to cross the broken bridge. Thiamine (a PDH cofactor) is also trialed.

mcqs.stem: A normal-to-low lactate:pyruvate ratio (rather than a high ratio) points away from which alternative?
choices: A respiratory-chain (ETC) defect, where impaired NADH reoxidation drives a HIGH lactate:pyruvate ratio
mcqs.explanation: ETC defects raise NADH, pushing pyruvate→lactate and giving a HIGH L:P ratio (>20-25). PDH deficiency blocks pyruvate use directly, so pyruvate AND lactate both rise with a relatively normal ratio — a useful discriminator.
